coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of hexadecylamine modified carbon dots and their friction-reducing and anti-wear performance in polyalphaolefin
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Zhu
Wang, Baogang
Luo, Mina
Lu, Hongsheng
coredata.dc:description: Hexadecylamine modified carbon dots (CDs-HA) were synthesized by pyrolyzing citric acid in HA. The friction-reducing and anti-wear properties of CDs-HA as lubricant additives of polyalphaolefin (PAO4) were evaluated on the universal mechanical tester under the ball-on-disk linear reciprocating model and steel/steel contact. The concentration (c) and load both displayed great influences on the tribological properties of CDs-HA. At the load of 20 N and the optimum c of 1 wt%, the friction coefficient and wear volume of PAO4 reduced by 27.1% and 45.9%, respectively. In the load range of 20–100 N, the friction-reducing performance of CDs-HA remarkably decayed with increasing load, whereas their anti-wear property attenuated tenderly. The results of wear scar analyses demonstrated that the CDs-HA as additives not only participated in the formation of boundary lubrication films on the rubbing surfaces, but also should play the rolling, polishing and mending effects, and hence exhibited the outstanding friction-reducing and anti-wear performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hexadecylamine
Carbon dot
Friction
Wear
Lubricant additives

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Tribological behaviors of CDs-HA in PAO4
The worn surface analyses and lubrication mechanism of CDs-HA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthetic process of CDs-HA.
(a) TEM and (b) high-resolution TEM images and (c) particle size distribution of CDs-HA.
FTIR spectra of (a) CA, (b) HA and (c) CDs-HA.
Optical photos of (a) PAO4 and (b–d) CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension (1 wt%) after preparation, standing for 1 month and 3 months, respectively.

(a) Friction coefficient curves of CDs-HA/PAO4 suspensions with different concentrations (c). (b) Mean friction coefficient and wear volume of CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension as a function of c (load: 40 N; sliding speed: 10 mm/s; duration: 20 min).
(a) Mean friction coefficient and wear volume of PAO and CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension (1 wt%) varying with the increasing load (load: 20–100 N; sliding speed: 10 mm/s; duration: 20 min).

(a) Friction coefficient curves (inset: the molecular structure of ZDDP), (b) mean friction coefficients and wear volumes of (A) PAO4, (B) HA (1 wt%), (C) ZDDP (1 wt%) solutions in PAO4, and (D) CDs-HA (1 wt%)/PAO4 suspension (load: 40 N; sliding speed: 10 mm/s; duration: 20 min).
(a) Friction coefficient curves, (b) mean friction coefficients and wear volumes of PAO4 and CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension (1 wt%) under a long duration of 200 min (load: 40 N; sliding speed: 10 mm/s).

Microscopic image and corresponding two-dimensional profile of the wear scar lubricated by (a) PAO4 and (b) CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension (1 wt%), respectively (load: 40 N; sliding speed: 10 mm/s; duration: 20 min).
The SEM and high-resolution SEM images of the wear scar lubricated by (a) PAO4 and (b) CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension (1 wt%), respectively (load: 40 N; sliding speed: 10 mm/s; duration: 20 min).

Elemental mappings (derived from the EDS analyses) of the wear scars lubricated by (a) PAO4 and (b) CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension (1 wt%), respectively (load: 40 N; sliding speed: 10 mm/s; duration: 20 min).

(a) Survey XPS spectra and elemental compositions of the wear scars lubricated by PAO4 and (b) CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension (1 wt%). (b–d) Fe2p, C1s and O1s fine XPS spectra of the wear scar lubricated by PAO4. (e–h) N1s, Fe2p, C1s and O1s fine XPS spectra of the wear scar lubricated by CDs-HA (1 wt%)/PAO4 suspension (load: 40 N; sliding speed: 10 mm/s; duration: 20 min).
Elemental compositions of wear scars lubricated by PAO4 and CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension (1 wt%), respectively (Load: 40 N; sliding speed: 10 mm/s; duration: 20 min).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of hexadecylamine modified carbon dots and their friction-reducing and anti-wear performance in polyalphaolefin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hexadecylamine modified carbon dots (CDs-HA) were synthesized by pyrolyzing citric acid in HA. The friction-reducing and anti-wear properties of CDs-HA as lubricant additives of polyalphaolefin (PAO4) were evaluated on the universal mechanical tester under the ball-on-disk linear reciprocating model and steel/steel contact. The concentration (c) and load both displayed great influences on the tribological properties of CDs-HA. At the load of 20 N and the optimum c of 1 wt%, the friction coefficient and wear volume of PAO4 reduced by 27.1% and 45.9%, respectively. In the load range of 20–100 N, the friction-reducing performance of CDs-HA remarkably decayed with increasing load, whereas their anti-wear property attenuated tenderly. The results of wear scar analyses demonstrated that the CDs-HA as additives not only participated in the formation of boundary lubrication films on the rubbing surfaces, but also should play the rolling, polishing and mending effects, and hence exhibited the outstanding friction-reducing and anti-wear performance.

ce:section.ce:para: When a car runs on the road, almost one-sixth of its energy provided by the fuel will go to overcoming the friction losses [1]. Undoubtedly, lubricants play indispensable roles in saving fuels and enhancing the durability and efficiency of machinery. As a most widely used lubricant in vehicles and industrial machinery, the fully formulated engine oil is usually composed of a base oil and many additives such as viscosity index improver, friction reducer, anti-wear agent and detergent [2]. Polyalphaolefin (PAO) has been the most common base oil for engine oil because of its high viscosity-temperature index, low pour point and volatility, and high thermal and oxidation stability [3]. Although their content is quite low, the additives are the quintessence of high-grade engine oil. Zinc dialkyldithiophosphates (ZDDPs) have always been used as the commercial friction-reducing and anti-wear additives of engine oils since the late 1930s because they not only work well under diverse operating conditions but also are reasonably priced [4]. However, the drawbacks of ZDDPs are increasingly prominent in terms of the modern oil formulations and environmental regulations [5]. On one hand, the friction-reducing performance of ZDDPs is unsatisfactory despite their anti-wear property is well. Moreover, the ZDDPs will lose their friction-reducing and anti-wear functions and even result in the degradation of engine oil when the temperature is higher than 160 °C [6]. On the other hand, the ZDDPs not only may cause damages to the catalysts in catalytic converters, but also can raise various environmental concerns due to the phosphorus and sulfur emissions [7]. Therefore, developing of environmentally benign lubricant additives as the alternatives of ZDDPs is definitely meaningful and urgent.

During the past two decades, lots of organic compounds (such as triphenyl phosphorothionate [8], trimeric silver pyrazolate complex [9], cyclopropane-carboxylic acid [10], alkyl-ethylene amines [11], borate esters [12], ionic liquids [13–16], and polymers [17]) and nanomaterials [18] have been used to replace ZDDPs. Therein, various nanomaterials, including metal nanoparticles [19], metallic and nonmetallic oxides [20–22], carbon nanoparticles [23], carbon nanotube [24], graphene [25–28], boron nitride composite [29], and graphene composites [30–34], have exhibited the greatest potential as the alternatives of ZDDPs owing to their excellent friction-reducing and anti-wear properties, high chemical and thermal stability, and environmental friendliness. One of the biggest obstacles towards practical application for the nanoadditives is their poor dispersion stability in PAO. Despite the above problem has been greatly improved by adding dispersants or surface modification, these treatment procedures are environmentally unfriendly and time-consuming. Thus, achieving the full potential of nanoadditives remains a great challenge.

In recent several years, the carbon dots (CDs) are becoming a promising nanoadditives as they possess many peculiarities of high-performance lubricant additives [35]. First, the particle size of CDs is usually small (<10 nm) and uniform, making them exhibit good embedding stability between the rubbing surfaces. Second, the CDs can display good dispersion stability in base oils because abundant groups are decorated onto their surfaces during their synthesis process [36–38]. Hence, adding dispersants or surface modification can be avoided. Moreover, the surface groups on CDs can be well designed with some special lubrication functions [39]. Third, the carbon resources used for synthesizing CDs are versatile and easily available. As a novel carbon material, the CDs exhibit high chemical and thermal stability, good environmental friendliness and unique self-lubricating effect. Unsurprisingly, the CDs as the additives of water and polyethylene glycol have shown distinguished friction-reducing and anti-wear performance [39–48]. However, the CDs have seldom been used as the additives of PAO because the researches on synthesis and purification of oil-soluble CDs are rare [49,50]. In addition, the previous oil-soluble CDs as additives were obtained by the two-step method, that is, the further surface modification treatments were needed after the synthesis processes, leading to the complex synthesis procedures, low modification rates and unsatisfactory dispersion stability in PAO.

Herein, hexadecylamine modified carbon dots (CDs-HA) were facile synthesized by the one-step method, i.e. directly pyrolyzing citric acid in HA. The HA groups on CDs-HA endowed them with favorable dispersion stability in PAO4. The tribological performance of CDs-HA as the lubricant additives of PAO4 was evaluated in detail on a universal mechanical tester (UMT) under ball-on-disk linear reciprocating model and steel/steel contact. The effects of additive concentration and load on the lubrication functions of CDs-HA were revealed. The service life of CDs-HA was investigated by prolonging the duration of friction. The surface morphologies and elemental compositions of wear scars on disks lubricated by PAO4 and CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension were probed by white light interferometer, scanning electron microscope equipped with the energy dispersive system, and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Finally, the lubrication mechanism of CDs-HA was discussed according to the results of friction tests and surface analyses.

Anhydrous citric acid (CA, >99.5%) and hexadecylamine (HA, >98%) were purchased from Aladdin Shanghai Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (China). Polyalphaolefin (PAO4) was obtained from Foshan Nahai Jinjia Trade Co., Ltd. (China). Anhydrous alcohol (AR) was purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (China). All the chemicals were used as received without further purification.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the CDs-HA were facile synthesized by a one-step pyrolysis method. Briefly, 2.10 g of CA and 4.82 g of HA were consecutively added into a 50 mL round flask and the flask was placed into an oil bath at room temperature. Then the oil bath was heated to 240 °C under magnetic stirring and Ar atmosphere and kept at this temperature for 4 h. After cooling down to the room temperature, the black gel-like product was washed with 20 mL of hot ethanol (50 °C) by high-speed centrifugation for six times to remove the by-products and remaining HA molecules. Finally, the purified product was placed into a vacuum oven at 70 °C overnight and the CDs-HA were obtained as the brown powder. Based on the carbon source of CA, the yield of CDs-HA was about 25.9 wt%.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a FEI Tecnai G20 system operating at 200 kV. The specimen was prepared by drop-casting the CDs-HA/n-hexane suspension (0.02 mg/mL) onto a carbon-coated copper grid, followed by drying at the room temperature. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a FTIR spectrophotometer (WQF-520) by the KBr pellet method. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDX) were performed on an SEM (EVO MA15) system. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250) with a mono-chromatized Al Ka X-ray source (1486.71 eV).

The tribological properties of PAO4 and CDs-HA/PAO4 suspensions were evaluated on a Bruker universal mechanical tester (UMT, America) under the ball-on-disk linear reciprocating model and steel/steel contact. Specifically, the friction test was performed by pressing an upper running steel ball (AISI52100, 12.7 mm in diameter, 59–61 HRC in hardness) over a lower stationary AISI52100 steel disk (linear velocity: 10 mm/s; amplitude: 5 mm; duration: 20–200 min; load: 20–100 N; room temperature). The width and depth of wear scar were measured using a white light interferometer. The friction coefficients were automatically recorded by a data-acquiring system linked to UMT. The wear volume of lower disk was calculated according to the longitudinal cross-sectional area and the length of the wear scar. The mean friction coefficient was calculated as the average value of all data points on a friction coefficient curve. Three replicate tests were conducted for each friction process.

As shown in Fig. 2a, the TEM image of CDs-HA confirms that the spherical CDs-HA have been successfully synthesized by the facile one-step pyrolysis method. No aggregates can be found on the carbon-coated copper grid, meaning that the CDs-HA are oil-soluble and almost mono-dispersed in n-hexane. The good lipophilicity of CDs-HA implies that the HA molecules are decorated onto the surfaces of CDs-HA during the synthesis process. The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2b) shows that the CDs-HA are almost amorphous because no lattice fringes can be found, which is a common phenomenon for the CDs synthesized by the bottom-up method [51]. The particle size distribution of CDs-HA (shown in Fig. 2c) gained by accounting more than 150 particles indicates that the CDs-HA are uniform with an average particle size of about 4.2 nm.

The FTIR spectrum of CDs-HA shown in Fig. 3c reveals the information of surface groups on CDs-HA. The weak bands at 3352 and 1770 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H/N-H and C=O of carboxyl groups, respectively, reflecting that a few oxygen-containing groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups are existent on the surfaces of CDs-HA. The strong bands at 2923 and 2855 cm−1 are derived from the C-H stretching vibrations of methyl and methylene groups, respectively. In addition, the bands of CDs-HA below 1500 cm−1 are similar to that of HA (Fig. 3b) but totally different from that of CA (Fig. 3a). There results demonstrate that the CA molecules are absolutely decomposed during the pyrolysis process, whereas the HA molecules are survived and attached onto the surfaces of CDs-HA. The feature bands at 1746 and 1706 cm−1 belonging to the carboxyl groups of CA and the typical band at 1569 cm−1 representing the amino groups of HA are all disappeared in the spectrum of CDs-HA. Meanwhile, a new band at 1705 cm−1 belonging to the C=O stretching vibrations of amide groups appears, meaning that the HA molecules are covalently decorated onto the surfaces of CDs-HA by amide groups. The abundant HA groups on CDs-HA give the reason why they possess good dispersion stability in n-hexane, which is of key importance for their practical application as the oil-soluble lubricant additives.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological behaviors of CDs-HA in PAO4
ce:section.ce:para: The long-term dispersion stability in the base oil is the prerequisite for the nanoadditives. Fig. 4 gives the optical photos of CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension (1 wt%) standing for different times. Just as expected, the oil-soluble CDs-HA exhibit excellent dispersion stability in the PAO4. The suspension can keep transparent and stable without any precipitates for at least 3 months. The good dispersion stability endows the CDs-HA with great potential to become a promising additives of PAO4. Hence, their tribological behaviors in PAO4 were investigated in detail.

Fig. 5a illustrates that the friction coefficient curves of CDs-HA/PAO4 suspensions with different concentrations (c) are obviously lower than that of PAO4, reflecting the remarkable friction-reducing function of CDs-HA. Fig. 5b demonstrates that the mean friction coefficient and wear volume of CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension both reduce first and then rise with the increasing c. In other words, an optimum c of 1.0 wt% is existent. At the optimum c, the mean friction coefficient and wear volume of CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension are 25.9% and 38.1% lower than that of PAO4, indicating that the CDs-HA as additives can greatly improve the friction-reducing and anti-wear performance of PAO4.

The effects of load on the tribological properties of CDs-HA in PAO4 were investigated at the c of 1 wt%. Fig. 6a shows that the friction-reducing performance of CDs-HA gradually attenuates when the load increases from 20 to 100 N. Specifically, the mean friction coefficient of the suspension is 27.1% and 6.9% lower than that of PAO4 at the load of 20 N and 100 N, respectively. While the anti-wear property of CDs-HA only mildly decays with the increasing load. Typically, the mean wear volume of the suspension is 45.9% and 29.6% lower than PAO4 at the load of 20 N and 100 N, respectively. The above phenomenon can be explained by the lubrication theory. Under the low load of, the CDs-HA play the effective rolling and polishing effects between the rubbing surfaces, whereas these effects is largely attenuated when the load is high. Hence, the friction-reducing performance of CDs-HA is greatly suppressed under the high load. The mending effects and film-forming ability of CDs-HA are always effective in the load range of 20–100 N and hence ensure their obvious anti-wear property. Generally, the CDs-HA have better lubrication function at relatively low load.

To illustrate the superiority of CDs-HA, the tribological properties of HA, ZDDP and CDs-HA as additives of PAO4 were compared under the same conditions. Fig. 7 shows the friction coefficient curve, the mean friction coefficient and wear volume of CDs-HA (1 wt%)/PAO4 suspension are obviously lower than that of HA (1 wt%) solution in PAO4, indicating that the friction-reducing and anti-wear performance of CDs-HA is much better than that of HA. The carbon cores and HA groups of CDs-HA should play the synergistic lubrication effect. Although the friction coefficient curve and mean friction coefficient of ZDDP (1 wt%) solution in PAO4 are slightly lower than that of CDs-HA suspension, the friction coefficient curve of CDs-HA suspension is smoother than that of ZDDP solution. Moreover, the mean wear volume of CDs-HA suspension is remarkably smaller than that of ZDDP solution. Generally, the CDs-HA exhibit better lubrication function than ZDDP.

The durability of CDs-HA as additives was also evaluated by prolonging the duration from 20 to 200 min. As shown in Fig. 8a, the whole friction coefficient curve of CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension (1 wt%) is much smoother and lower than that of PAO4. Correspondingly, the mean friction coefficient and wear volume of suspension (shown in Fig. 8b) is 20.4% and 33.6% lower than that of PAO4, respectively, suggesting that the CDs-HA as additives have a long service life.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The worn surface analyses and lubrication mechanism of CDs-HA
ce:section.ce:para: To disclose the friction-reducing and anti-wear mechanisms of CDs-HA as additives, the worn surfaces lubricated by PAO4 and CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension (1 wt%) were analyzed by a series of characterization technologies. Fig. 9 gives the microscopic images and two-dimensional profiles of wear scars lubricated by PAO4 and CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension (1 wt%). The wear scars lubricated by PAO4 and suspension are similar in width, whereas the depth of the wear scar lubricated by suspension is much shallower than that of PAO4. In addition, it can also be found that the scratches on the wear scar lubricated by suspension are remarkably relieved. These results directly verify the outstanding anti-wear property of CDs-HA.

The SEM images in Fig. 10 show that the wear scar lubricated by suspension is much smoother than that of PAO4, which is well consistent with the results in Fig. 9. In addition, the high-resolution SEM image (Fig. 10a) reveals that severe scuffing and lots of pits can be observed on the wear scar lubricated by PAO4, whereas they are disappeared on the wear scar lubricated by suspension. This phenomenon demonstrates that the severe adhesive wear should occur on the wear scar lubricated by PAO4. While the CDs-HA should greatly alleviate the above adhesive wear by the rolling, mending and polishing effects [52].

The elemental mappings and compositions of wear scars lubricated by PAO4 and CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension (1 wt%) were gained by EDS analyses and shown in Fig. 11 and Table 1 . Two points need to be noted. First, the O and Fe contents are higher on the wear scar lubricated by PAO4, meaning that the wear scar is oxidized severely. Second, the C content is much higher and a low amount of N element appears on the wear scar lubricated by the suspension, implying that the CDs-HA should gradually deposit onto the wear scar during the friction process to participate in the formation of boundary lubrication film. This film can not only play an important role in reducing friction and wear, but also prevent the worn surface from severe oxidation.

As its detection depth is much shallower than that of EDS analysis, the XPS analysis is more able to probe the information of boundary lubrication film on the wear scar. Fig. 12a shows the elemental species and compositions of the wear scars lubricated by PAO4 and CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension (1 wt%). The much higher C and N contents but much low O content on the wear scar lubricated by suspension further verify that the CDs-HA should directly participate in the formation of boundary lubrication films. Fig. 12b–h show the fine XPS spectra of various elements on the wear scars lubricated by PAO4 and suspension. The Fe2p XPS spectra indicate that the Fe element on the wear scars is mainly existent in the form of oxides. In comparison with the wear scar lubricated by PAO4, the C, O and N species on the wear scar lubricated by suspension are more abundant owing to the existence of embedded CDs-HA.

Base on the above characterization results, the lubrication mechanism of CDs-HA as additives was put forward. During the friction process, the CDs-HA can quickly enter into the asperities of rubbing surfaces and exhibit good embedding stability owing to their small particle size and good dispersion stability. Under the complex effects of high pressure and frictional heat, the boundary lubrication films composed of iron oxides and CDs-HA is gradually formed on rubbing surfaces by absorption and deposition. These films not only avoid direct contact but also alleviate the oxidation of rubbing surfaces. Meanwhile, the CDs-HA should also play the rolling, mending and polishing effects on the rubbing surfaces. The above merits derived from the CDs-HA corporately result in smooth wear scar, low friction coefficient and small wear volume.

The CDs-HA were successfully synthesized by pyrolyzing CA in HA. The CDs-HA exhibited good compatibility with PAO4 owing to their abundant HA surface groups. As the lubricant additives of PAO4, the CDs-HA showed outstanding friction-reducing and anti-wear performance. Typically, at the load of 20 N and optimum c of 1 wt%, the mean coefficient and wear volume of CDs-HA/PAO4 suspension is 27.1% and 45.9% lower than that of PAO4, respectively. The anti-wear property of CDs-HA was always obvious with the increasing load from 20 to 100 N despite their friction-reducing function was sharply decayed. The lubrication functions of CDs-HA could keep stable when the duration was prolonged from 20 to 200 min, reflecting a long service life of CDs-HA. The results of worn surface analyses demonstrated that the CDs-HA not only participated in the formation of boundary lubrication films but also might play the rolling, polishing and mending effects on rubbing surfaces, directly accounting for their outstanding friction-reducing and anti-wear performance. Considering their good comparability in PAO4 and long service life, the CDs-HA are expected to become a promising lubrication additives of PAO4.
Zhu Liang: acquisition of data, analysis of data, and writing the draft. Baogang Wang, Mina Luo and Hongsheng Lu: design of investigation, interpretation of data, review and editing the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51975493) and the Postdoctoral Science Foundation Funded Project of China (2015M582567).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: U.D. Schwarz, Tracking antiwear film formation, Science 348 (2015) 40-41.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tribological performance of nanoparticles as lubricating oil additives
